past_medical_history: Chronic low back pain with two lumbar epidural steroid injections (ages 35 and 37)
Irritable bowel syndrome diagnosed at age 28
Generalized anxiety disorder diagnosed at age 25
Major depressive episode at age 34, treated with sertraline, in partial remission
Childhood history of physical abuse (documented in psychiatric records)
Chronic migraine without aura, approximately 8 headache days per month

medications.indication: Muscle spasm related to chronic pain

medications.frequency: As needed for migraine, up to 9 per month
As needed, up to three times daily

family_history: Father with type 2 diabetes and hypertension
Maternal aunt with multiple sclerosis, diagnosed at age 45
Paternal grandfather with Parkinson's disease, onset in late 70s
One sister (age 37) with irritable bowel syndrome and anxiety
No family history of epilepsy, stroke, or neuromuscular disease

social_history.occupation: Former administrative assistant, on disability leave for 6 months due to progressive weakness
social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine, approximately once weekly, less recently due to medications
social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, quit 5 years ago, approximately 8 pack-year history
social_history.recreational_drugs: Denies current use; remote marijuana use in early 20s
social_history.living_situation: Lives with her husband and two children (ages 8 and 12) in a single-story home. Husband has become primary caregiver. Currently using a wheelchair full-time at home.
social_history.exercise: Previously active, walked 3-4 miles daily until approximately 8 months ago when weakness began. Now unable to ambulate independently.
social_history.psychosocial_stressors: Husband recently lost his job 10 months ago, significant financial stress. Her mother was diagnosed with breast cancer 11 months ago. Patient describes feeling overwhelmed and helpless prior to symptom onset.

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Affect is flat but cooperative. Speech is fluent without dysarthria or aphasia. Naming, repetition, and comprehension intact. Attention normal on digit span (6 forward, 4 backward). Memory 3/3 registration, 2/3 recall at 5 minutes (recalled third with category cue). Clock drawing normal. Fund of knowledge appropriate for education level. No evidence of thought disorder.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3 mm to 2 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus or diplopia. Facial sensation intact bilaterally to light touch and pinprick. Face symmetric at rest and with activation. Hearing intact bilaterally to finger rub. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Upper extremities: Normal bulk and tone. Strength 5/5 bilaterally in deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors/flexors, and grip. No pronator drift. Lower extremities: Normal bulk. Tone is normal without spasticity or rigidity. On formal testing, hip flexors 2/5 bilaterally, knee extensors 3/5 bilaterally, ankle dorsiflexors 3/5 bilaterally, ankle plantarflexors 2/5 bilaterally. However, strength testing demonstrates give-way pattern with initial full effort followed by sudden collapse. When the patient is distracted during conversation, spontaneous leg movements are observed including repositioning legs in bed with apparently greater force than demonstrated on formal testing. No muscle atrophy. No fasciculations.
neurological_exam.sensory: Reports complete loss of sensation to light touch, pinprick, temperature, proprioception, and vibration from the bilateral inguinal creases distally in a non-dermatomal distribution. The sensory loss has a sharp midline split at the trunk. Vibration sense is reported absent over the legs but is felt normally at the sternum and over the iliac crests. Double simultaneous stimulation testing is normal in upper extremities.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Upper extremities: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, and brachioradialis bilaterally. Lower extremities: 2+ and symmetric at patellae and Achilles bilaterally. Plantar responses are flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus. No Hoffman sign.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Rapid alternating movements normal in upper extremities. Heel-to-shin testing is difficult to assess as patient reports inability to lift legs adequately, but when performed with examiner assistance, no dysmetria is apparent.
neurological_exam.gait: Patient states she is unable to stand or walk. When assisted to standing with two-person support, she demonstrates marked sway with irregular, non-patterned truncal movements. She reaches for support but catches herself with movements that require significant strength and coordination. She does not fall when unexpectedly released (with spotter present). When asked to walk, she takes several steps with a dragging, non-pyramidal pattern of bilateral leg movement, inconsistent with any recognized neurological gait pattern.
neurological_exam.additional: Hoover sign was tested and is equivocal — involuntary hip extension is weak on the right but questionably present on the left during contralateral hip flexion against resistance. Tremor entrainment test: patient has intermittent coarse bilateral hand tremor at rest that is variable in frequency and amplitude; when asked to perform a rhythmic tapping task with the right hand, the left hand tremor entrains to the tapping frequency briefly before stopping entirely. The tremor is distractible — it ceases when the patient is engaged in conversation about her children.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive bilateral leg weakness over 8 months, now wheelchair-dependent, with intermittent tremor and episodes of whole-body shaking
patient.history_present_illness:
Mrs. Patterson is a 40-year-old right-handed woman with a complex medical history including fibromyalgia, chronic pain, anxiety, and depression, who is admitted for evaluation of progressive bilateral lower extremity weakness that has rendered her wheelchair-dependent over the past 8 months. She reports that the weakness began insidiously approximately 8 months ago with a sensation of heaviness in both legs, initially more prominent on the right. Over the ensuing weeks, she noticed increasing difficulty climbing stairs, followed by difficulty walking on flat surfaces. By three months into the illness, she required a cane, and by five months she transitioned to a wheelchair, which she now uses full-time.

She describes the weakness as constant but fluctuating in severity from day to day, with some days being slightly better than others without clear pattern. She denies any preceding infection, vaccination, or trauma. She also reports complete numbness in both legs from the groin down, describing it as though her legs do not belong to her. She reports episodes of whole-body shaking lasting 5 to 15 minutes that began approximately 4 months ago, occurring 2 to 3 times per week. During these episodes she reports retained awareness and ability to hear those around her, though she sometimes closes her eyes. Her husband states the shaking is irregular and appears to vary in intensity. On one occasion he tried gently holding her arms during an episode and the shaking stopped momentarily before resuming in her legs.

She has also developed an intermittent coarse tremor in both hands over the past 2 months, which she notices primarily when she is anxious or fatigued. She has seen multiple physicians over the past 8 months. An outpatient neurologist obtained a brain and spine MRI which were normal, routine nerve conduction studies that were normal, and basic blood work that was unremarkable. She was referred with a tentative diagnosis of possible multiple sclerosis versus inflammatory myelopathy versus motor neuron disease, though all workup has been negative. A rheumatology consultation attributed her symptoms to severe fibromyalgia, but the referring neurologist felt the degree of weakness was disproportionate to that diagnosis and referred her for inpatient evaluation. The patient is frustrated with the lack of a diagnosis and expresses fear that she has a progressive neurodegenerative disease. She tearfully states that her life has changed completely and she feels she is a burden to her family.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz well-organized alpha rhythm, symmetric, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Brief drowsiness achieved with vertex waves; stage II sleep with K-complexes and sleep spindles

findings.state: Wakefulness, drowsiness, and stage II sleep
findings.clinical_correlation: No epileptiform activity identified during the entire recording, including during two clinical events (see below).

findings.type: Clinical event captured - episode of bilateral shaking
findings.morphology: During a 7-minute episode of bilateral upper and lower extremity shaking with eyes closed, the EEG showed normal waking background rhythm with muscle artifact. No ictal EEG pattern, no post-ictal slowing, no rhythmic evolution. Normal background persisted throughout the event.
findings.clinical_correlation: Clinical event with bilateral shaking has no electrographic correlate. This is consistent with a psychogenic non-epileptic seizure (PNES). The presence of normal alpha rhythm throughout the event excludes generalized epileptic seizure. Focal onset seizure without surface EEG correlate is possible but extremely unlikely given the bilateral motor manifestations.

findings.type: Clinical event captured - brief tremor episode
findings.morphology: During a 2-minute episode of bilateral hand tremor, EEG shows only low-amplitude muscle artifact superimposed on a normal background. No rhythmic ictal pattern.
findings.clinical_correlation: Bilateral hand tremor without EEG correlate, consistent with functional tremor.

artifacts.impact: Moderate artifact during shaking episodes, but underlying cerebral activity clearly identifiable throughout, confirming absence of epileptiform correlate

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Three minutes performed. No change in background or induction of clinical symptoms. No epileptiform discharges.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal EEG recording including during two captured clinical events (one episode of bilateral shaking lasting 7 minutes and one episode of bilateral hand tremor lasting 2 minutes). Both events had no electrographic correlate, with preserved normal waking background throughout. These findings are consistent with psychogenic non-epileptic seizures and functional tremor. No epileptiform discharges. No focal slowing. Normal background.
eeg.limitations: Routine recording duration. Muscle artifact during shaking episode partially obscured some channels but underlying cerebral activity remained identifiable.
recommended_actions: Correlate with clinical history and neurological examination
Consider prolonged video-EEG monitoring if additional events need characterization
Evaluate for functional neurological disorder given absence of EEG correlate during clinical events

signal_characteristics.T2: Decreased signal at L4-L5 and L5-S1 discs consistent with early desiccation

findings.mass_effect: No significant disc protrusion. No neural foraminal stenosis. No spinal canal stenosis.

additional_observations: No evidence of demyelinating disease (no periventricular, juxtacortical, or infratentorial lesions meeting McDonald criteria)
Mild lumbar disc desiccation is an age-appropriate incidental finding and does not explain the clinical presentation of bilateral leg weakness
Conus medullaris terminates at L1 level, normal position
No evidence of myelopathy or cord lesion
mri.impression: Normal brain MRI with contrast. Normal cervical spinal cord. Lumbar spine shows mild age-appropriate disc desiccation at L4-L5 and L5-S1 without significant herniation, stenosis, or neural impingement. No structural lesion to explain progressive bilateral lower extremity weakness. No evidence of demyelinating disease, myelopathy, mass lesion, or motor neuron disease.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: High - no structural or inflammatory CNS pathology identified
High - age-appropriate finding, does not explain bilateral weakness

recommended_actions: Correlate with clinical examination and electrodiagnostic studies
Normal imaging does not exclude all neurological diagnoses; clinical correlation essential
If demyelinating disease remains a concern, consider repeat imaging in 3-6 months

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm with no acute abnormalities.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Normal ECG. No cardiac cause for syncope or episodic symptoms.

Thyroid_and_Metabolic.clinical_significance: Mildly low vitamin D, consistent with known deficiency on supplementation. Does not explain neurological symptoms.
Normal CK argues against active myopathy or muscle inflammation
Normal, argues against Wilson disease or copper deficiency myelopathy

CSF_Screening.clinical_significance: Argues against neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder

labs.interpretation: Comprehensive laboratory evaluation is essentially unremarkable. CBC and BMP entirely normal. Inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP) normal, autoimmune panel (ANA, anti-dsDNA, ANCA) negative. Thyroid function normal. Vitamin B12, folate, copper, and ceruloplasmin normal. CK normal, arguing against myopathy. NMO-IgG and MOG antibodies negative. The only abnormality is mildly low vitamin D (22 ng/mL), consistent with known deficiency on supplementation, which does not explain the clinical presentation. This extensively normal workup is a significant finding in itself, as it excludes many organic causes of bilateral leg weakness.
abnormal_values_summary: Vitamin D (25-OH) 22 ng/mL - mildly low, known deficiency on supplementation, does not explain neurological symptoms

background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves, K-complexes, and sleep spindles. All sleep stages recorded over 3-day monitoring.

findings.type: Clinical event captured - prolonged bilateral shaking episode
findings.frequency: Four events captured over 3 days (durations: 8 min, 12 min, 6 min, 15 min)
findings.morphology: During all four events, EEG showed persistent normal waking alpha rhythm with superimposed muscle artifact. No ictal pattern, no rhythmic evolution, no post-ictal slowing. Eyes were closed during events; when eyes briefly opened, normal reactive alpha was observed.
findings.clinical_correlation: Four habitual clinical events captured, all without electrographic correlate. Video review shows asynchronous, irregular bilateral limb shaking with waxing and waning intensity, side-to-side head movement, and eye closure. Events stopped abruptly when patient believed she was unobserved during one episode. Findings are diagnostic of psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES).

findings.morphology: Variable-frequency bilateral hand tremor with no EEG correlate. Tremor ceased during distraction tasks on two occasions.
findings.clinical_correlation: Functional tremor confirmed by distractibility and entrainment.

artifacts.impact: Moderate artifact but underlying cerebral rhythms clearly identifiable throughout all events, confirming absence of epileptiform correlate

output.impression: Normal 3-day continuous video-EEG monitoring. Four habitual episodes of bilateral shaking captured, all without electrographic correlate, diagnostic of psychogenic non-epileptic seizures. Multiple episodes of functional tremor also captured with variable frequency and distractibility. No epileptiform discharges. No electrographic seizures. Normal background throughout. These findings, combined with the clinical features, are strongly supportive of functional neurological disorder.
output.limitations: None significant. Adequate number of habitual events captured for diagnostic certainty.
recommended_actions: Communicate diagnosis of psychogenic non-epileptic seizures to patient with empathy and clarity
Evaluate for functional neurological disorder as the unifying diagnosis
Psychiatry or psychology referral for psychotherapy

Nerve_Conduction_Studies.clinical_significance: Distal latency 4.2ms, amplitude 5.8mV, conduction velocity 48 m/s. Normal.
Distal latency 4.0ms, amplitude 6.1mV, conduction velocity 49 m/s. Normal.
Distal latency 4.5ms, amplitude 8.2mV, conduction velocity 46 m/s. Normal.
Distal latency 4.3ms, amplitude 7.9mV, conduction velocity 47 m/s. Normal.
Right: amplitude 12.4 microV, CV 48 m/s. Left: amplitude 11.8 microV, CV 47 m/s. Normal bilaterally.
Right: amplitude 14.2 microV, CV 49 m/s. Left: amplitude 13.8 microV, CV 48 m/s. Normal bilaterally.

Needle_EMG.clinical_significance: No fibrillations, positive sharp waves, or fasciculations. Normal MUAP morphology and recruitment.
No fibrillations or positive sharp waves. Normal MUAP morphology. Recruitment initially appeared reduced but normalized when patient was distracted.
Normal insertional activity, no spontaneous activity, normal MUAPs.
No fibrillations or positive sharp waves in lumbar paraspinal muscles, arguing against radiculopathy or motor neuron disease.

output.interpretation: Normal comprehensive electrodiagnostic study of bilateral lower extremities. Motor and sensory nerve conduction studies are entirely normal, excluding peripheral neuropathy, polyradiculopathy, and demyelinating neuropathy (including Guillain-Barre syndrome and CIDP). Needle EMG shows no evidence of denervation, myopathy, or motor neuron disease. Notably, recruitment in the right vastus medialis was initially reduced but normalized with distraction, a finding consistent with functional weakness rather than true neurogenic weakness. Normal electrodiagnostic findings in the setting of severe clinical weakness is a significant discordance that supports a functional etiology.

findings.location: Cervical spinal cord (repeat imaging for completeness)

signal_characteristics.T2: Decreased disc signal consistent with desiccation, unchanged from prior

findings.mass_effect: No significant disc protrusion. No central canal stenosis. No neural foraminal narrowing.

additional_observations: No evidence of myelopathy at any level
No demyelinating lesions in the spinal cord
No leptomeningeal enhancement to suggest carcinomatous meningitis or sarcoidosis
Normal conus medullaris at L1 level
Lumbar disc desiccation unchanged, does not explain clinical presentation
output.impression: Normal cervical and thoracic spinal cord MRI with contrast. No evidence of myelopathy, demyelinating disease, cord compression, or spinal cord lesion to explain bilateral lower extremity weakness. Mild lumbar disc desiccation unchanged and clinically insignificant. Complete spinal neuraxis imaging is normal.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: High - no structural or inflammatory pathology

recommended_actions: Normal spinal imaging effectively excludes myelopathy, structural cord lesion, and spinal demyelinating disease as causes of bilateral leg weakness
Clinical correlation with electrodiagnostic studies and examination findings is recommended

Neuropsychological_Testing.clinical_significance: Low average processing speed, may reflect the impact of pain, fatigue, depression, or medication effects (pregabalin, duloxetine) rather than primary cognitive deficit
Moderate depression severity despite current duloxetine treatment
Passes symptom validity testing, suggesting adequate effort and not consistent with malingering

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological testing reveals average cognitive functioning across most domains with low average processing speed, likely attributable to medication effects, chronic pain, and depression rather than a primary neurological cognitive deficit. Mood testing reveals moderate depression and severe anxiety, which are not adequately controlled on current medication regimen. Importantly, the patient passes symptom validity testing (TOMM 48/50), indicating adequate effort and arguing against malingering. The profile is consistent with functional neurological disorder with comorbid depression and anxiety, rather than a neurodegenerative or structural neurological process.
abnormal_values_summary: Processing Speed Index 88 - low average, likely secondary to medications, pain, and depression
Beck Depression Inventory-II score 28 - moderate depression
Beck Anxiety Inventory score 32 - severe anxiety
Symptom Validity Testing passed - not consistent with malingering

Psychiatric_Evaluation.value: Functional Neurological Symptom Disorder (Conversion Disorder) with weakness, abnormal movement, and attacks/seizures; Major Depressive Disorder, recurrent, moderate; Generalized Anxiety Disorder; PTSD features related to childhood trauma
Psychiatric_Evaluation.clinical_significance: Multiple comorbid psychiatric diagnoses identified. History of childhood physical abuse is a significant predisposing factor for functional neurological disorder.

Psychiatric_Evaluation.reference_range: 0-4 none, 5-9 mild, 10-14 moderate, 15-19 moderately severe, 20-27 severe
Psychiatric_Evaluation.clinical_significance: Moderately severe depression, suboptimally treated on duloxetine 60 mg
Score exceeds PTSD screening threshold. Patient reports intrusive memories, avoidance of reminders, hypervigilance, and sleep disturbance related to childhood physical abuse.
Elevated dissociative experiences, below pathological threshold but notably above average. Dissociation is a recognized risk factor and maintaining factor in functional neurological disorder.

output.interpretation: Comprehensive psychiatric evaluation reveals significant comorbidity including moderately severe depression (suboptimally treated), severe anxiety, probable PTSD related to childhood physical abuse, and elevated dissociative experiences. The history of childhood trauma, current psychosocial stressors (husband's job loss, mother's cancer diagnosis), and the temporal relationship between stressor accumulation and symptom onset are consistent with predisposing and precipitating factors for functional neurological disorder. The patient does not demonstrate features of factitious disorder or malingering; she is genuinely distressed by her symptoms. Recommendations: (1) optimize antidepressant therapy, (2) begin trauma-focused psychotherapy (CPT or EMDR), (3) consider CBT specific to FND, (4) coordinate with neurology for integrated treatment approach.
abnormal_values_summary: PCL-5 score 42 - positive PTSD screen, related to childhood trauma

CSF_Analysis.clinical_significance: No evidence of intrathecal immunoglobulin synthesis, argues against multiple sclerosis

output.interpretation: Entirely normal CSF analysis. No evidence of inflammation, infection, demyelination, or malignancy. Absent oligoclonal bands and normal IgG index effectively exclude multiple sclerosis. Normal opening pressure excludes idiopathic intracranial hypertension. This normal result further supports the absence of organic CNS disease and is consistent with functional neurological disorder.

output.query: functional neurological disorder conversion disorder positive diagnostic signs

results.title: Functional neurological disorder: new subtypes and shared mechanisms
results.source: Espay et al., The Lancet Neurology 2018;17(1):84-98
results.summary: FND should be diagnosed by positive clinical signs (Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness) rather than by exclusion of organic disease. The diagnosis carries the same diagnostic certainty as other neurological conditions when positive signs are present.

results.title: Diagnostic criteria for functional neurological disorder (conversion disorder) in the DSM-5
results.summary: DSM-5 requires positive clinical findings of incompatibility between symptoms and recognized neurological or medical conditions. The diagnosis should not be made solely by exclusion. Key positive signs include Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness, and non-dermatomal sensory loss.

results.title: Outcomes and prognosis of functional neurological disorder
results.source: Gelauff et al., Journal of Neurology 2019;266:1878-1890
results.summary: Prognosis is variable but generally better with early diagnosis, clear communication of the diagnosis, and multidisciplinary treatment including physiotherapy and psychotherapy. Delay in diagnosis is associated with worse outcomes. Approximately 40% of patients show significant improvement with appropriate treatment.

results.title: The role of physiotherapy in functional neurological disorder
results.source: Nielsen et al., Journal of Neurology, Neurosurgery & Psychiatry 2015;86(10):1113-1119
results.summary: Specialized physiotherapy for FND focuses on movement retraining, distraction-based techniques, and graded activity. Evidence supports consensus-based physiotherapy as a first-line treatment for functional motor symptoms.

output.summary: Functional neurological disorder (conversion disorder) should be diagnosed based on positive clinical signs demonstrating internal inconsistency rather than solely by exclusion of organic disease. Key positive signs include Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness, distractibility of symptoms, and non-dermatomal sensory patterns. The DSM-5 emphasizes positive diagnostic criteria. Treatment involves multidisciplinary care with specialized physiotherapy, psychotherapy (especially for comorbid trauma and depression), and clear, empathic communication of the diagnosis. Early diagnosis and treatment are associated with better outcomes.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Functional neurological disorder (conversion disorder) with weakness, abnormal movements (functional tremor), and psychogenic non-epileptic seizures

differential.likelihood: Low - initially moderate due to family history (maternal aunt with MS) and progressive bilateral weakness, but normal brain and spinal cord MRI, absent oligoclonal bands, negative NMO-IgG and MOG antibodies, and absence of dissemination in space and time effectively exclude this diagnosis
differential.key_features: Would expect white matter lesions on MRI, oligoclonal bands in CSF, clinical dissemination in space and time. Family history raises pre-test probability but all objective testing is negative.

differential.likelihood: Low - normal spinal cord MRI with no signal abnormality, negative NMO-IgG, negative MOG antibody, and normal CSF exclude this diagnosis
differential.key_features: Would expect spinal cord lesion on MRI, CSF pleocytosis, positive aquaporin-4 or MOG antibodies. All negative.

differential.likelihood: Very low - initially concerning due to progressive weakness, but normal EMG with no denervation, no fasciculations, normal upper motor neuron signs (no hyperreflexia, no Babinski), and inconsistent weakness pattern exclude this
differential.key_features: Would expect combination of upper and lower motor neuron signs, fasciculations, progressive and non-fluctuating weakness, EMG showing denervation. None present.

differential.likelihood: Low - normal nerve conduction studies and EMG effectively exclude this diagnosis
differential.key_features: Would expect prolonged distal latencies, reduced conduction velocities, temporal dispersion on NCS. All nerve conduction studies normal.

differential.likelihood: Very low - no fatigable weakness, no ocular or bulbar symptoms, normal CK, and strength pattern inconsistent with neuromuscular junction disorder
differential.key_features: Would expect fatigable weakness, ptosis, diplopia, bulbar symptoms. No such features present.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive history including detailed characterization of weakness progression, associated symptoms (tremor, episodes of shaking), psychosocial stressors, trauma history, and psychiatric history
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Progressive bilateral weakness with fluctuation, co-occurring functional tremor and episodes of shaking with retained awareness, significant psychosocial stressors, childhood trauma history, and comorbid fibromyalgia/anxiety/depression - all raising suspicion for functional etiology

optimal_actions.action: Perform detailed neurological examination specifically looking for positive signs of functional neurological disorder including Hoover sign, give-way weakness, tremor entrainment, distractibility of symptoms, and inconsistency between exam findings and observed function
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Give-way weakness, inconsistency between formal testing and observed spontaneous movements, non-dermatomal sensory loss with midline splitting, distractible tremor with entrainment, equivocal Hoover sign - subtle positive functional signs that may not be immediately obvious

optimal_actions.action: Order EEG to evaluate episodes of shaking for epileptiform correlate
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG with captured clinical events showing no electrographic correlate, diagnostic of psychogenic non-epileptic seizures

optimal_actions.action: Order brain and spine MRI with contrast to exclude structural and inflammatory CNS disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal brain and spinal cord imaging, excluding demyelination, myelopathy, mass lesion, and structural causes of weakness

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, inflammatory markers, autoimmune panel, thyroid function, B12, copper, CK, and demyelinating antibodies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Essentially normal comprehensive workup, excluding metabolic, inflammatory, autoimmune, and myopathic causes of weakness

optimal_actions.action: Order EMG and nerve conduction studies to evaluate for neuromuscular causes of weakness
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal NCS and EMG excluding neuropathy, myopathy, and motor neuron disease. Recruitment pattern normalizes with distraction, supporting functional weakness.

optimal_actions.action: Diagnose functional neurological disorder based on positive clinical signs (give-way weakness, inconsistency, distractible tremor, non-epileptic seizures) supported by negative workup, rather than diagnosing solely by exclusion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Diagnosis of FND based on convergence of positive signs and negative workup

optimal_actions.action: Communicate diagnosis to patient with empathy, using validated communication strategies: explain what the condition IS (not just what it is not), validate symptoms as real, explain the mechanism, and provide hope for recovery
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient understands that FND is a real neurological condition involving abnormal nervous system function, not imagined or fabricated symptoms

optimal_actions.action: Refer for specialized physiotherapy for functional motor symptoms and psychotherapy (trauma-focused) for underlying psychological contributors
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Initiation of multidisciplinary treatment plan including FND-specific physiotherapy and trauma-focused psychotherapy

optimal_actions.action: Optimize psychiatric medication management for comorbid depression and anxiety
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Review and potentially adjust duloxetine dose or add adjunctive treatment for suboptimally managed depression and anxiety

critical_actions: Recognize positive signs of functional neurological disorder (give-way weakness, inconsistency between formal testing and observed function, distractible tremor with entrainment, non-dermatomal sensory loss) rather than relying solely on exclusion of organic disease
Perform adequate workup to rule out treatable organic disease before making the diagnosis, including MRI, EMG/NCS, and laboratory studies
Capture clinical events on EEG to diagnose psychogenic non-epileptic seizures and exclude epilepsy
Communicate the diagnosis to the patient with empathy, clarity, and validation that symptoms are real — this is therapeutic in itself
Do not label the patient as malingering or suggest symptoms are fabricated
Refer for multidisciplinary treatment including specialized physiotherapy and psychotherapy
Assess and address comorbid psychiatric conditions (depression, anxiety, PTSD) that are maintaining factors
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing the patient with multiple sclerosis, motor neuron disease, or inflammatory myelopathy based solely on progressive weakness without supporting objective evidence
Attributing the lumbar disc desiccation on MRI as the cause of bilateral leg weakness (incidental age-appropriate finding, does not explain the clinical picture)
Prescribing antiepileptic drugs for the shaking episodes which are psychogenic non-epileptic seizures, not epileptic seizures
Telling the patient that nothing is wrong or that symptoms are in her head — this is harmful and inaccurate
Suggesting the patient is malingering or faking — symptom validity testing is passed and she is genuinely distressed
Ordering unnecessary invasive procedures (e.g., spinal cord biopsy, repeat lumbar punctures) without clinical indication
Starting immunosuppressive therapy (e.g., steroids, IVIG, plasma exchange) for a presumed inflammatory condition without objective evidence
Performing unnecessary surgical interventions on the lumbar spine based on incidental disc findings
key_reasoning_points: Functional neurological disorder should be diagnosed by positive clinical signs (DSM-5 criteria), not merely by exclusion of organic disease. The key positive signs in this case include give-way weakness, inconsistency between formal testing and spontaneous movements, distractible and entrainable tremor, non-dermatomal sensory loss with midline splitting, and psychogenic non-epileptic seizures on video-EEG.

The diagnostic puzzle difficulty arises because positive functional signs are initially subtle and equivocal (Hoover sign is equivocal, give-way weakness could be attributed to pain), requiring careful serial examination and video-EEG to establish the diagnosis with confidence.

The extensive negative workup (normal MRI brain and spine, normal EMG/NCS, normal labs, negative autoimmune markers) is important to exclude organic disease but should not be the sole basis for diagnosing FND.
The patient's family history of MS (maternal aunt) is a red herring that could inappropriately anchor the clinician toward a demyelinating diagnosis despite absence of supportive evidence.
The lumbar disc desiccation on MRI is an incidental age-appropriate finding that does not explain bilateral leg weakness or sensory loss and should not be attributed as the cause.
Comorbid fibromyalgia, chronic pain, depression, anxiety, and childhood trauma are recognized predisposing and maintaining factors for FND, not reasons to dismiss the neurological presentation.
The patient passes symptom validity testing (TOMM), which distinguishes FND from malingering — symptoms are involuntary and the patient is not fabricating.
The temporal relationship between accumulating psychosocial stressors (husband's job loss, mother's cancer diagnosis) and symptom onset is consistent with the biopsychosocial model of FND.

Successful treatment requires clear, empathic communication of the diagnosis, specialized physiotherapy for functional motor symptoms, and psychotherapy addressing underlying trauma and psychiatric comorbidity.

metadata.author: NeuroBench case generator
metadata.sub_domain: Functional weakness with PNES - diagnostic puzzle
metadata.estimated_complexity: Diagnostic puzzle - progressive bilateral weakness mimicking organic neurological disease, initially equivocal positive functional signs, extensive negative workup needed, comorbid psychiatric factors, family history red herring for MS
red_herrings: Maternal aunt with multiple sclerosis raising suspicion for demyelinating disease
Lumbar disc desiccation on MRI that could be misattributed as cause of weakness
History of fibromyalgia that could lead to premature attribution of symptoms to fibromyalgia without adequate neurological workup
Mildly low vitamin D level which does not explain the neurological presentation
Equivocal Hoover sign initially making positive functional signs less obvious
Complex pain history with multiple prior evaluations creating anchoring bias toward organic disease
